fuzzy_prd: We need a static HTTP server that makes a directory tree on disk browsable as web pages, so a developer can serve a folder with a single command and let anyone navigate its contents in a browser. Two specific behaviors make the listing genuinely useful. First, every folder page should show a ready-to-paste shell command at the bottom that a visitor can run to mirror the entire sub-folder recursively — this command should use the wget download tool with a fixed set of flags that handle recursion, timestamps, avoiding index files, and not creating host-named subdirectories. The exact form of the command changes depending on how deep the current folder sits below the root: at root level add a destination flag naming the server's authority, one level deep needs no path-adjustment, and two or more levels deep should add a flag that strips the right number of leading path segments. Second, when the listing is requested in a flat/raw mode, the navigational links should be wired so that clicking into a sub-directory keeps the visitor in flat mode, but links to actual files point straight at the file without any mode modifier. The parent-directory link (shown when not at the root) should also stay in flat mode. Handle link construction consistently with how the existing renderer builds other listing links. Directory links always end with a trailing slash on their path.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: pub fn create_recursive_download_link(host: &str, path: &str) -> String; pub fn list_directory_raw(path: &Path, base: &Path) -> Vec<Entry>

trigger_keywords: what is the exact wget command format
what flags does the recursive download command use
what is the fixed flag block in the download command
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The wget command always starts with 'wget -rcnHp -R 'index.html*'' (fixed flags), then a depth-dependent path argument, then the URL in single quotes ending with '?raw=true'. The flags mean: -r recursive, -c continue partial, -n no clobber, -H span hosts, -p page requisites, -R reject index.html*.

trigger_keywords: what flag is used at depth 0 vs depth 1 vs depth 2+
how does the command vary by folder depth
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Depth 0 (root): add " -P '<authority>'" (e.g., -P 'localhost:8080'). Depth 1 (one segment below root): no path-adjustment flag. Depth 2+: add " --cut-dirs=<depth-1>" (e.g., at depth 2: --cut-dirs=1, at depth 3: --cut-dirs=2). Depth is the number of path segments below root.

trigger_keywords: what are the link roles in the raw listing
what link role names appear in the flat listing output
what is the role of the parent directory link
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Three link roles: 'root' (parent-directory link, shown only when not at served root), 'directory' (sub-directory links), 'file' (regular file links). Each output line is '<role> <url>'. Only directory-type links (root and directory) append '?raw=true'; file links do not.

trigger_keywords: what is the parent directory link target in raw mode
what URL does the root/parent link point to
how is the parent directory link formatted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parent directory link (role 'root') points to '../?raw=true' (relative URL, trailing slash, then ?raw=true). It is only shown when the requested folder is not the served root.

trigger_keywords: what fixed host authority does the adapter use
what is the server address used in the test adapter
what host and port are embedded in the download command URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test adapter uses the fixed host authority 'localhost:8080'. The absolute URL in the wget command is built as 'http://localhost:8080<path>?raw=true'. The -P flag also uses 'localhost:8080'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle link construction consistently with how the existing renderer builds other listing links
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The raw listing link logic mirrors the behavior in src/renderer.rs where entry links are constructed: directory entries get '?raw=true' appended with trailing slash on path, file entries get plain paths. The link roles (root/directory/file) correspond to the CSS class attributes used in the existing renderer.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_recursive_download_command.json.description: Server renders wget command in footer for recursive folder download. Command varies by depth below root.

feature2_raw_listing_links.json.description: In raw/flat listing mode, directory links keep ?raw=true, file links do not. Parent link shown when not at root.
